grounding.text:
<!-- image -->

##### TEXT MINING

##### 2023-2024 EXAM SAMPLE QUESTIONS

##### 1. Sample questions based on LECTURES content

(Multiple-Choice) One of the problems of Natural Languages is that different sentences can have the same meaning. This is known as the problem of:

- a. Overfitting
- b. Ambiguity
- c. Variability
- d. Generalization

##### Answer: c

(Open, development question) Explain how the self-attention mechanism works inside a Transformer.

Answer: The self-attention mechanism in a Transformer works by calculating attention scores between every pair of words in the input sequence. To compute these attention scores, three types of embeddings are generated for each word: query, key,  and  value.  The  attention  scores  are  calculated  by  taking  the  dot  product  between  the  query  and  key  embeddings, followed by applying a softmax function to obtain a probability distribution. These attention scores determine how much each word should contribute to the final representation of the current word. Finally, the attention-weighted sum of the value embeddings, weighted by the attention scores, is computed. This weighted sum serves as the contextual representation of the current word, capturing the relevant information from the entire sequence.

##### 2. Sample questions based on LABS content

(Multiple-Choice) What is the purpose of Lemmatization in Text Mining?

- a. Removing stop words from the text
- b. Reducing words to their root form
- c. Grouping words
grounding.citation: 2024 EXAM TM SAMPLE QUESTIONS

Introduction to NLP

Definition:

Natural language processing (NLP) is an area of computer science and artificial intelligence that is concerned with the interaction between computers and humans in natural language. The ultimate goal of NLP is to enable computers to understand language as well as we do (or better ☺ ) .

concepts.title: Challenges of Natural Language
concepts.summary: Natural language presents challenges such as grammatical variability, the existence of paraphrases (same meaning, different wording), and ambiguity, which must often be resolved using context.

concepts.title: NLP System Limitations
concepts.summary: NLP systems can face issues when applied outside their training domain (Out-of-Domain or OOD) or when encountering words they have never seen before (Out-of-Vocabulary or OOV).

concepts.title: Corpus and Data Splitting
concepts.summary: A corpus is a collection of organized text datasets, and the initial step in NLP pipelines involves splitting this corpus into Train, Validation, and Test sets, often using 80%/10%/10% splits for small corpora.

concepts.title: Feature Engineering (Bag-of-Words)
concepts.summary: Bag-of-Words is a feature engineering technique where documents are represented as sparse vectors, with each word in the vocabulary serving as a feature.

concepts.title: Text Preprocessing Methods
concepts.summary: Text preprocessing involves several techniques to clean and standardize text data before analysis, such as removing stop words, converting case, and normalizing entities.

Curse of Dimensionality

Text Preprocessing Methods

Text Preprocessing Methods

Less tokens same meaning!

Stop Words

Text Preprocessing Methods

Stop Words

Some words are not very informative , and their presence only represents more rules and/or more processing . Thus, the first thing we can do is to get rid of them.

Stop Words

Examples: on, the, a, of, from, and, that, it, in (…)

Stop Words

'Who is the main actor of Pulp Fiction'

Stop Words

- [who, main, actor, Pulp Fiction]

Stop Words

'What actor played the main role in Pulp Fiction'

Stop Words

- [what, main, actor, Pulp Fiction, played, role]

Text Preprocessing Methods

Converting everything to lowercase also helps reducing the vocabulary size .  Examples:

Text Preprocessing Methods

```
"Cats sleep more than cats play."  ->        [Cats, sleep, more, than, cats, play] ->  #words = 6


                "Cats sleep more than cats play."  ->        [cats, sleep

concepts.summary: Text normalization uses techniques like regular expressions to standardize variations in data such as dates, names, and numbers, or to replace them with generic tokens for anonymization.

Text 2 - ' I live in France'

The Bag-of-Words size equals the size of the vocabulary!

Text 2, in = 1. Text 2, live = 1. Text 2, love = 0. Text 2, Paris = 0. Text 2, (…) = …. Text 2, word n-1 = 0. Text 2, word n = 0

Curse of Dimensionality

Text Preprocessing Methods

Text Preprocessing Methods

Less tokens same meaning!

Stop Words

Text Preprocessing Methods

Stop Words

Some words are not very informative , and their presence only represents more rules and/or more processing . Thus, the first thing we can do is to get rid of them.

Stop Words

Examples: on, the, a, of, from, and, that, it, in (…)

Stop Words

'Who is the main actor of Pulp Fiction'

Stop Words

- [who, main, actor, Pulp Fiction]

Stop Words

'What actor played the main role in Pulp Fiction'

Stop Words

- [what, main, actor, Pulp Fiction, played, role]

Text Preprocessing Methods

Converting everything to lowercase also helps reducing the vocabulary size .  Examples:

Text Preprocessing Methods

```
"Cats sleep more than cats play."  ->        [Cats, sleep, more, than, cats, play] ->  #words = 6


                "Cats sleep more than cats play."  ->        [cats, sleep

concepts.title: Stemming/ Lemmatization
concepts.summary: These are text preprocessing methods used to reduce inflection in language so that words representing the same meaning are captured by the same token.

grounding.text:
Text Normalization / Regular Expressions

$$D ( \text {Text 1, Text 2} ) = \sqrt { ( 1 - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 - 0 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 - 0 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 0 - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 - 1 ) ^ { 2 } } = 1 . 7 3 \left ( < 2 . 2 4 \right )$$

Text Normalization / Regular Expressions

Text Preprocessing Methods

Stemming/ Lemmatization

Text Preprocessing Methods

Stemming/ Lemmatization

Transform words in a way that if they represent the same meaning they are captured by the same token. Reduce inflection in language (Inflected Language)

Stemming/ Lemmatization

- Stemming is the process of removing the last few characters of a given word, to obtain a shorter form, even if that form doesn't have any meaning.

Stemming/ Lemmatization

Ex: Change, changing, changes, changer becomes chang

Stemming/ Lemmatization

- Lemmatization is the process of turning words into their root word .
- Ex: Change, changing, changes, changer becomes change

concepts.title: N-Grams
concepts.summary: An n-gram is a contiguous sequence of n items from a given sample, which can be characters, words, or lemmas. They are used in text mining, often as an extension of the Bag of Words model.

concepts.title: Problems with Bag of Words (BoW)
concepts.summary: The Bag of Words model has several limitations, including the curse of dimensionality, the discarding of word order (no semantic relationships), and the assumption that all words have equal importance.

concepts.title: Problems with N-grams
concepts.summary: N-grams suffer from data sparseness, especially when increasing N, because they do not handle long-distance dependencies well, leading to many zero counts in the BoW representation.

concepts.title: TF-IDF
concepts.summary: TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency) is a weighting scheme used to evaluate how important a word is to a document in a collection. It is calculated by multiplying the Term Frequency (TF) by the Inverse Document Frequency (IDF).

concepts.title: Term Frequency (TF)
concepts.summary: TF measures how often a specific term appears within a given document. It is calculated as the raw count of the term in the document.

grounding.text:
Term Frequency -Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF)

= number of documents where the term appears Doc 1 (d1) - 'The dog is on the table'

Term Frequency -Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF)

Doc 2 (d2) - ' The cat is on the table'

Term Frequency -Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF)

TF('word', d) - Count 'word' in document d

Term Frequency -Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF)

IDF('word', D) -log(|D|/n t ), where |D| is the number of documents in the corpus (in this example, we have 2 documents) and n t is the number of documents n with the word t

concepts.title: Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)
concepts.summary: IDF measures how important a term is across an entire corpus. It is calculated using the formula log(|D|/n_t), where |D| is the total number of documents and n_t is the number of documents containing the term t.

Distance Metrics 03.

- Given 2 documents we can transform them into feature space and compare them with simple distance metrics .
- The feature space can be binary values or raw counts or TFIDF of any kind of N-grams.
- If they share a lot of features, they will be close to each other .

Euclidean Distance

The Euclidean Distance is the most common use of distance. When data is dense or continuous , this is the best proximity measure.

Euclidean Distance

Distance Metrics 03.

- Given 2 documents we can transform them into feature space and compare them with simple distance metrics .
- The feature space can be binary values or raw counts or TFIDF of any kind of N-grams.
- If they share a lot of features, they will be close to each other .

Euclidean Distance

The Euclidean Distance is the most common use of distance. When data is dense or continuous , this is the best proximity measure.

concepts.title: Cosine Distance
concepts.summary: Cosine Distance is a metric used to calculate the distance between vectors, which can be applied to phonetics (words) or orthography (words and documents).

grounding.text:
Cosine Distance:

$$\left | | v 2 | \right | = \sqrt { 0 ^ { 2 } + 0 ^ { 2 } + \dots + 0 . 3 ^ { 2 } } = 0 . 3$$

Cosine Distance

Distance Metrics

Cosine Distance

2 ways to calculate distance through phonetics:

Cosine Distance

Phonetics -words

Cosine Distance

Orthography -words and documents

Phonetics

Distance Metrics

Phonetics

Soundex is a phonetic algorithm for indexing names by sound, as pronounced in  English.

Phonetics

Example:

Phonetics

Smith  -> S530

Phonetics

Smythe -> S530

Phonetics

George -> G620

Phonetics

Garage -> G620

Phonetics

Minimum Edit Distance (Levenshtein) counts the minimum number of operations required to transform one string into the other.

Phonetics

- Insertion of a single symbol. If a = uv , then inserting the symbol x produces uxv . This can also be denoted ε→ x , using ε to  denote the empty string.
- Deletion of a single symbol changes uxv to uv ( x →ε ).
- Substitution of a single symbol x for a symbol y ≠ x changes uxv to uyv ( x → y )

Jaccard Coefficient

Distance Metrics

Jaccard Coefficient

Jaccard coefficient measures similarity between finite sample sets, and is defined as the size of the intersection divided by the size of the union of the sample sets:

Jaccard Coefficient

$$J ( A , B ) = \frac { | A \cap B | } { | A \cup B | }$$

Sørensen -Dice is similar to Jaccard but strings with different lengths are not so strongly penalized

Doc 1 (d1) - 'The dog is on the table'

Sørensen -Dice is similar to Jaccard but s

concepts.title: Problems with Bag of Words (BoW)
concepts.summary: BoW representations suffer from the curse of dimensionality, discard word order, treat all words equally, and fail to capture how context changes meaning.

concepts.title: Term-Term Matrix
concepts.summary: A Term-Term matrix has dimensions |V|x|V|, where each cell counts the co-occurrence of a row word (target) and a column word (context) in the corpus.

concepts.title: Point-wise Mutual Information (PMI)
concepts.summary: PMI measures how often two events occur relative to what would be expected if they were independent, defined by the formula I(x, y) = log_2 [ P(x, y) / (P(x)P(y)) ].

concepts.title: Positive PMI (PPMI)
concepts.summary: PPMI is a variation of PMI that replaces all negative PMI values with zero, calculated as PPMI(w, c) = max( log_2 [ P(w, c) / (P(w)P(c)) ], 0 ).

concepts.title: Word Embeddings
concepts.summary: Word embeddings are dense vector representations of words learned by training shallow neural models, such as Word2Vec (Skip-gram). They allow models to capture semantic relationships between words.

grounding.text:
Sequential Models Text Mining 2025-2026

Bruno Jardim

Sequential Models Text Mining 2025-2026

bjardim@novaims.unl.pt

Lecture Plan

01. Review of Word Embeddings
02. Recurrent Neural Networks
03. Long Short-Term Memory Neural Networks
04. Sequence-to-Sequence Models

Lecture Plan

Review of Word Embeddings 01.

Word2Vec (Skip-gram)

Word2Vec learns dense vector representations of words by training a shallow neural model to predict a target word from surrounding words (Skip-gram). Words used in similar contexts end up with similar vectors, enabling semantic relationships such as similarity and analogies.

Word2Vec (Skip-gram)

After training the model, the embeddings are stored in the Weights Matrix (W) of the network In the example below, Master can be represented as low dimensionality vector (an embedding): [0.445, 0.897, 0.966]

Word Embeddings Properties

After trained, Word Embeddings can extract semantic relationships between words

concepts.title: Common RNN Tasks
concepts.summary: RNNs are used for various sequence-based tasks, including next word prediction, sequence labeling, sequence classification, and machine translation.

grounding.text:
Common Tasks

- -Next word prediction
- -Sequence Labeling
- -Sequence Classification
- -Machine Translation

The Architecture behind RNNs

Recurrent Neural Networks

The Architecture behind RNNs

A recurrent neural network (RNN) is a network that contains a cycle within its network connections, meaning that the value of some unit is directly, or indirectly, dependent on its own earlier outputs as an input.

The Architecture behind RNNs

$$\begin{array} { l c l } { { h _ { t } } } & { = } & { { g ( U h _ { t - 1 } + W x _ { t } ) } } \\ { { y _ { t } } } & { = } & { { f ( V h _ { t } ) } } \end{array}$$

The Architecture behind RNNs

Recurrent Neural Networks

The Architecture behind RNNs

The matrices U, V and W are shared across time, while new values for h and y are calculated with each time step

The Architecture behind RNNs

$$\begin{array} { c c c } h _ { 0 } \leftarrow 0 \\ \text {for } l \leftarrow 1 \text { to } L \text {LENGTH} ( x ) \text { do } \\ h _ { i } \leftarrow g ( U h _ { i - 1 } + W x _ { i } ) \\ y _ { i } \leftarrow f ( V h _ { i } ) \end{array}$$

The Architecture behind RNNs

Recurrent Neural Networks

The Architecture behind RNNs

The matrices U, V and W are shared across time, while new values for h and y are calculated with each time step

The Architecture behind RNNs

concepts.title: RNN Processing Flow
concepts.summary: For a sentence, each word embedding is processed sequentially through the network, and the hidden vector from the previous word is used to calculate the hidden vector for the next word.

concepts.title: RNN Limitations
concepts.summary: RNNs suffer from issues like Long Dependencies, where the final hidden state may not reflect early parts of the sequence, and Vanishing Gradients during training.

The Architecture behind RNNs

The Architecture behind RNNs

A recurrent neural network (RNN) is a network that contains a cycle within its network connections, meaning that the value of some unit is directly, or indirectly, dependent on its own earlier outputs as an input.

The Architecture behind RNNs

$$\begin{array} { l c l } { { h _ { t } } } & { = } & { { g ( U h _ { t - 1 } + W x _ { t } ) } } \\ { { y _ { t } } } & { = } & { { f ( V h _ { t } ) } } \end{array}$$

concepts.title: LSTM Cell State Calculation
concepts.summary: The cell state is updated by pointwise multiplying the previous cell state by the forget vector, and then adding the output of the input gate multiplied by the candidate cell state.

grounding.text:
2.5. Calculate Cell State

3.  Output Gate

The Architecture behind LSTMs

Long Short-Term Memory Neural Networks

The Architecture behind LSTMs

Long Short-Term Memory Neural Networks

The Architecture behind LSTMs

$$C _ { t } = f _ { t } * C _ { t - 1 } + i _ { t } * \tilde { C } _ { t }$$

2.5. Calculate the Cell State

1. The cell state gets pointwise multiplied by the forget vector . This has a possibility of dropping values in the cell state if it gets multiplied by values near 0.
2. Then we take the output from the input gate and do a pointwise addition which updates the cell state to new values that the neural network finds relevant.

The Architecture behind LSTMs

Long Short-Term Memory Neural Networks

Gates in LSTM:

1. Forget Gate
2. Input Gate
3. 2.5. Calculate Cell State
3.  Output Gate

The Architecture behind LSTMs

Long Short-Term Memory Neural Networks

The Architecture behind LSTMs

Long Short-Term Memory Neural Networks

The Architecture behind LSTMs

$$o _ { t } & = \sigma \left ( W _ { o } \ [ h _ { t - 1 } , x _ { t } ] \ + \ b _ { o } \right ) \\ h _ { t } & = o _ { t } * \tanh \left ( C _ { t } \right )$$

3. Output Gate

This gate decides what is the next hidden state or, if we are at the last step of the network, what is our prediction.

3. Output Gate

1. First, we pass the previous hidden state and the current input into a sigmoid function .
2. Then we pass the newly modified cell state to the tanh function .
3. We multiply the tanh output with t

concepts.title: Seq2Seq Decoder Architecture
concepts.summary: The decoder network in a Seq2Seq model is typically an RNN that takes the previous output element and the previous hidden state as input at each time step.

grounding.text:
The Architecture behind Seq2Seq

The decoder network is also typically some RNN. At each time step, the decoder takes the previous element of the output sequence as input, along with the previous hidden state of the decoder.

The Architecture behind Seq2Seq

The input to the decoder at time t is typically the previously generated element of the output sequence, y t-1 which is typically represented as a one-hot vector.

The Architecture behind Seq2Seq

Each recurrent unit accepts a hidden state from the previous unit and produces an output as well as its own hidden state.

The Architecture behind Seq2Seq

$$h _ { t } = f ( W ^ { ( h h ) } h _ { t - 1 } )$$

The Architecture behind Seq2Seq

Sequence-to-Sequence Models

The Architecture behind Seq2Seq

The output is calculated using the hidden state at the current time step together with the respective weight matrix .

The Architecture behind Seq2Seq

Softmax is used to create a probability vector which will help us determine the final output (e.g. word in the question-answering problem).

The Architecture behind Seq2Seq

$$y _ { t } = s o f t m a x ( W ^ { S } h _ { t } )$$

concepts.title: RNN Architecture
concepts.summary: The core RNN calculation involves updating the hidden state ($h_t$) using the previous hidden state ($h_{t-1}$) and the current input ($x_t$), and then generating an output ($y_t$) from the new hidden state.

Lecture Plan

Review of Seq2Seq Models 01.

The Architecture behind RNNs

Recurrent Neural Networks

The Architecture behind RNNs

To process sequential input , we can use some well know sequential models, like Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) and Long Short Term Memory Neural Networks (LSTM)

Common Tasks

- -Next word prediction
- -Sequence Labeling
- -Sequence Classification
- -Machine Translation

The Architecture behind RNNs

A Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) is a network that contains a cycle within its network connections, meaning that the value of some unit is directly, or indirectly, dependent on its own earlier outputs as an input.

The Architecture behind RNNs

$$\begin{array} { l c l } { { h _ { t } } } & { = } & { g ( U h _ { t - 1 } + W x _ { t } ) } \\ { { y _ { t } } } & { = } & { f ( V h _ { t } ) } \end{array}$$

The Architecture behind RNNs

Recurrent Neural Networks

The Architecture behind RNNs

The matrices U, V and W are shared across time, while new values for h and y are calculated with each time step

The Architecture behind RNNs

concepts.title: Sequence-to-Sequence Models (Seq2Seq)
concepts.summary: Seq2Seq models, including RNNs and LSTMs, are used for tasks involving sequential input and output, such as machine translation and next word prediction.

concepts.title: Attention Mechanism
concepts.summary: The attention mechanism addresses a limitation of classical encoder-decoder RNNs by allowing the decoder to focus on relevant parts of the source sentence rather than relying solely on the final hidden state vector.

concepts.title: Bottleneck Problem in RNNs
concepts.summary: Classical encoder-decoder RNNs force all source sentence information into a single fixed-length vector, which acts as a bottleneck for the decoder.

grounding.text:
How Models Learn What to Focus On

Let's say your source sentence is 50 words long. The first word of the English translation is probably highly correlated with the first word of the source sentence. But that means the decoder has to consider information from 50 steps ago, and that information needs to be somehow encoded in the vector .

How Models Learn What to Focus On

Attention Mechanism

How Models Learn What to Focus On

In summary, classical encoder-decoder RNN have to encode all information about a sentence into a single vector and then the decoder must produce a translation based on only that vector .

How Models Learn What to Focus On

Attention Mechanism

How Models Learn What to Focus On

This final hidden state from the encoder is acting as a bottleneck : it must represent absolutely everything about the meaning of the source text, since the only thing the decoder knows about the source text is what's in this context vector .

How Models Learn What to Focus On

Attention Mechanism

How Models Learn What to Focus On

Languages structure information differently ; important semantic information does not appear at the same point in a sentence across languages .

How Models Learn What to Focus On

In some languages, such as English, the verb appears early, allowing listeners or models to quickly understand the action being performed. In others, such as Korean or certain Dutch constructions, the verb appears at the end of the sentence, forcing the listener or model to
grounding.citation: How Models Learn What to Focus On > STEP 1

concepts.title: Attention Mechanism Solution
concepts.summary: The attention mechanism solves the bottleneck problem by allowing the decoder to access information from all encoder hidden states, not just the final one.

concepts.title: Attention as Weighted Sum
concepts.summary: The core idea of attention is to create a dynamic context vector by taking a weighted sum of all the encoder hidden states.

concepts.title: Language Structure Variation
concepts.summary: Languages structure information differently; the placement of semantic information, like verbs, varies across languages (e.g., early in English vs. late in Korean).

concepts.title: Attention Computation Steps
concepts.summary: The attention mechanism involves scoring each encoder state relative to the current decoder state, applying a softmax function to these scores, and then using these scores to weight the encoder states.

concepts.title: Dot Product Attention
concepts.summary: Dot product attention is a specific method used to capture relevance by computing a score measure between the current decoder state and each encoder state.

concepts.title: Attention Mechanism Steps
concepts.summary: The attention mechanism involves calculating similarity scores via dot product, normalizing these scores using softmax to create weights, and finally computing a context vector as a weighted average of encoder hidden states.

grounding.text:
How Models Learn What to Focus On

Attention Mechanism

How Models Learn What to Focus On

Attention Mechanism

STEP 2

The score that results from this dot product is a scalar that reflects the degree of similarity between the two vectors.

STEP 2

To make use of these scores, we'll normalize them with a softmax to create a vector of weights , α ij , that tells us the proportional relevance of each encoder hidden state j to the prior hidden decoder state h d i-1

STEP 2

$$\alpha _ { i j } \, = \, \text {softmax} ( s o r e ( { h } _ { i - 1 } ^ { d } , { h } _ { j } ^ { e } ) \, \forall j \in e )$$

How Models Learn What to Focus On

Attention Mechanism

But how to compute this dynamic context for each decoder time step?

Finally, given the distribution in α , we can compute a fixed-length context vector for the current decoder state by taking a weighted average over all the encoder hidden states.

But how to compute this dynamic context for each decoder time step?

$$c _ { i } \, = \, \sum _ { j } \alpha _ { i j } \, { h } _ { j } ^ { e }$$

How Models Learn What to Focus On

Attention Mechanism

How Models Learn What to Focus On

Attention Mechanism

How Models Learn What to Focus On

Attention Mechanism

We need to learn the context vector

concepts.summary: Transformers were introduced to overcome the sequential processing limitations of RNN-based models, allowing for parallel computation suitable for modern hardware like GPUs.

concepts.title: Transformer Origin and Mechanism
concepts.summary: Transformers capture long-distance relationships like LSTMs but achieve this without relying on recurrent connections. They utilize self-attention to allow each token to directly attend to all others, enabling parallel computation.

grounding.text:
The origin of Transformers

The standard architecture for building modern large language models is the Transformer .

The origin of Transformers

Like LSTMs, Transformers can capture long-distance relationships between words in a sequence. However, unlike LSTMs, Transformers do not rely on recurrent connections that process tokens step by step .

The origin of Transformers

Instead, Transformers use self-attention , a mechanism that allows each token to directly attend to all other tokens in the sequence. This makes it possible to compute token representations in parallel , improving training efficiency and making much better use of GPUs .

The Architecture behind Transformers

Transformer Architecture

The Architecture behind Transformers

Transformers are made of two main types of blocks : encoders and decoders.

The Architecture behind Transformers

The Encoder reads the input sequence and transforms each token into a contextual representation .

The Architecture behind Transformers

The Decoder uses these representations to generate the output sequence , one token at a time.

The Architecture behind Transformers

In the original Transformer architecture, the encoder and decoder work together: the encoder represents the input, and the decoder produces the output based on both the previous generated tokens and the encoder's representations.

The Architecture behind Transformers

Transformer Architecture

Transformers are made of two main types of blocks : Encoder and Decode

concepts.title: Transformer Architecture Components
concepts.summary: Transformers are built from Encoder and Decoder blocks. The Encoder processes the input sequence into contextual representations, while the Decoder uses these representations to generate the output sequence token by token.

concepts.title: Encoder and Decoder Layers
concepts.summary: Encoder layers contain self-attention and a standard Neural Network, moving information forward. Decoder layers incorporate self-attention, encoder-decoder attention, and a feed-forward neural network.

concepts.title: Encoder-Only Models
concepts.summary: Some Transformers use only the encoder to understand text rather than generate new text. These models read the entire sentence at once, producing rich, context-aware representations useful for transfer learning tasks.

concepts.title: BERT Model Architecture
concepts.summary: BERT is a famous Encoder-only Transformer model developed by Google, featuring specific architectural components like encoder layers, attention heads, and hidden size.

grounding.text:
Generating Word Representations with Transformers

BERT (Bidirecional Encoder Representations from Transformers), developed by Google, is probably the most famous Encoder-only Transformer model.

Generating Word Representations with Transformers

BERT (BERTLarge ) model uses 24 encoder layers , referred to in the paper as Transformer blocks.

Generating Word Representations with Transformers

BERT uses 16 self-attention heads , allowing the model to capture multiple contextual relationships in parallel.

Generating Word Representations with Transformers

It has a hidden size of 1024 units , making its internal representations larger and more expressive. This means that each token is represented by a 1024dimensional contextual embedding produced by the final encoder layer.

Generating Word Representations with Transformers

The representations generated by BERT can then be fed to another model as word embeddings

Training BERT

Transformer Encoders

Training BERT

Transfer learning is the process of reusing knowledge learned from one task to improve performance on another task .

Training BERT

In NLP, models such as BERT are first pre-trained on massive text corpora to learn general language patterns . They are called Pre-trained Language Models (PLMs)

Training BERT

This models can be called Foundation models - very large pre-trained models trained on broad and diverse datasets at massive scale .

concepts.title: BERT Training Process
concepts.summary: BERT is trained in a two-step process: initial pre-training on unlabeled data using self-supervised tasks, followed by fine-tuning on smaller labeled datasets for specific downstream tasks.

concepts.title: Self-Supervised Tasks in BERT
concepts.summary: BERT learns language patterns using tasks like Masked Language Modeling (MLM), where it predicts masked words, and Next Sentence Prediction (NSP), where it predicts sentence relationships.

grounding.text:
Sentence Embeddings

During self-attention, [CLS] can attend to all words in the sentence, while all words can also attend to [CLS]. Across multiple Transformer layers, the [CLS] embedding gradually becomes a contextual summary of the entire sequence .

Sentence Embeddings

During pre-training, objectives such as Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) , but also during fine tuning , we can explicitly force [CLS] to capture sentence-level information .

Sentence Embeddings

After the final encoder layer, BERT outputs one contextual embedding per token . For sequence classification tasks, the final embedding corresponding to [CLS] is usually selected as the sentence representation .

Sentence Embeddings

This vector is then fed into a simple classifier , typically a linear layer followed by Softmax or a Logistic Regression.

Transformer Architecture 01.

The origin of Transformers

Transformer Architecture

The origin of Transformers

Deep learning increasingly relied on GPUs (Graphics Processing Unit) because they can perform many computations in parallel. 1

The origin of Transformers

However, RNN-based language models process tokens one word at a time , with each hidden state depending on the previous one, so computation must proceed sequentially .

The origin of Transformers

This can be very time-consuming and limits parallel processing, meaning GPUs cannot fully use their many cores during training.

The origin of Transformers

Transformer Architecture

The origin of Transformers

Transformer Architecture

The origin of Transformers

To solve this issue Self-attention was formally introduced in the landmark 2017 paper titled 'Attention Is All You Need' by Vaswani et al. (2017)

The origin of Transformers

Self-attention allows each token to directly attend to all other tokens in the sequence at the same time , capturing dependencies regardless of distance

The origin of Transformers

The authors proposed a new model called 'Transformer' , that uses Self-at

concepts.title: Transformer Architecture Components
concepts.summary: The Transformer architecture includes self-attention layers within both Encoder and Decoder blocks. The Decoder also features an encoder-decoder attention layer, allowing it to attend to the encoder's outputs.

grounding.text:
Transformer Architecture

Inside each Encoder and Decoder , we will find a layer of self-attention and a standard Neural Network -information moves forward through the encoders.

Transformer Architecture

In the Decoder , we can also find a layer of encoder -decoder attention (where the decoder attends to the outputs of the encoder),

Encoder-only models read the full sentence at once, capturing context from both previous and following words .

Generating Word Representations with Transformers

They produce rich, context-aware word and sentence representations , usually stronger than static embeddings like Word2Vec or GloVe.

These representations can be reused in downstream tasks such as classification, sentiment analysis, NER, similarity, clustering, and retrieval ( Transfer Learning ).

concepts.title: BERT Training Process
concepts.summary: BERT training involves a two-step process: initial pretraining, followed by fine-tuning for a specific downstream task.

grounding.text:
BERT training is a two-step process :

1. first pretraining a model (which is already done when we use it)
2. and then fine-tuning it for a particular task (there are a lot of models already fine tuned in hugging face for ex.)
3. Finally, we can fine-tune it for a particular task, like sentiment analysis or predicting movie reviews

Transformer Encoders

Alammar, J., & Grootendorst, M. (2024). Hands-On large language models: Language Understanding and Generation . Sebastopol : O'Reilly Media.

Training BERT

Transformer Encoders

Training BERT

Over the years, many variations of BERT have been developed, including RoBERTa, DistilBERT, ALBERT, and XLM-RoBERTa, each trained in various contexts.

Training BERT

Classification with Encoders 03.

Word Embeddings

Classification with Encoders

Word Embeddings

Since BERT produces multiple representations for the same word across layers, we must choose how to extract the final embedding.

Word Embeddings

To extract embeddings for each word, instead of a sentence level one, we can aggregate information from all token embeddings using pooling strategies.

Common approaches include:

- Last Layer: use the final hidden representation of each token.
- Sum all layers :sum the token representations from all Transformer layers.
- Sum last four : sum the token representations from the final four layers.
- Concat last four : join the final four token representations into one larger embedding vector.

Classification with Encoders

*Learn more

BERT training is a two-step process :

1. first pretraining a model (which is already done when we use it)
2. and then fine-tuning it for a particular task (there are a lot of models already fine tuned in hugging face for ex.)
3. Finally, we can fine-tune it for a particular task, like sentiment analysis or predicting movie reviews

Transformer Encoders

Alammar, J., & Grootendorst, M. (2024). Hands-On large language models: Language Understanding and Generation . Sebastopol : O'Reilly Media.

Training BERT

Transformer Encoders

Training BERT

Over the years, many variations of BERT have been developed, including RoBERTa, DistilBERT, ALBERT, and XLM-RoBERTa, each trained in various contexts.

Training BERT

Classification with Encoders 03.

Word Embeddings

Classification with Encoders

Word Embeddings

Since BERT produces multiple representations for the same word across layers, we must choose how to extract the final embedding.

Word Embeddings

To extract embeddings for each word, instead of a sentence level one, we can aggregate information from all token embeddings using pooling strategies.

Common approaches include:

- Last Layer: use the final hidden representation of each token.
- Sum all layers :sum the token representations from all Transformer layers.
- Sum last four : sum the token representations from the final four layers.
- Concat last four : join the final four token representations into one larger embedding vector.

Classification with Encoders

concepts.title: BERT Classification Workflow
concepts.summary: Transformers like DistilBERT can generate token and sentence-level embeddings, which are then used as features in a standard ML classification task.

grounding.text:
Classification with BERT

Transformers (in this case DistilBERT) can be used to generate token embeddings as well as sentence-level embeddings -> through the CLS token, for example After obtaining sentence embeddings, we can use them as our features , which lets us proceed just as we would in a typical ML task

Classification with BERT

Classification with Encoders Once sentence embeddings have been generated, they can serve as our features , allowing the workflow to continue in much the same way as a standard ML task The next step is to train a classification model (for example, Logistic Regression) so the relationship between the sentence embeddings (reviews) and the target label (sentiment) can be learned

Classification with BERT

Classification with Encoders

For each new (unclassified) review, we:

- 1) create embeddings using DistilBERT and
- 2) pass the resulting sentence embeddings through our trained Logistic Regression model, which returns the probability associated with each label

Classification with Encoders

Self-Attention 04.

How does Self-Attention work?

Self-Attention

How does Self-Attention work?

Like LSTMs, Transformers can capture long-distance relationships between words in a sequence. However, unlike LSTMs, Transformers do not rely on recurrent connections that process tokens step by step .

How does Self-Attention work?

Instead, Transformers use Self-Attention , a mechanism that allows each token to directly attend to all other tokens in the seque

concepts.title: QKV Projection
concepts.summary: Transformers use weight matrices to project each input vector into representations for Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V).

grounding.text:
How does Self-Attention work?

To capture these three different roles, transformers introduce weight matrices used to project each input vector 𝑥𝑖 into a representation of its role as a key (K) , query (Q) , or value (V) .

How does Self-Attention work?

$$q _ { i } = W ^ { Q } x _ { i } ; \ k _ { i } = W ^ { K } x _ { i } ; \ v _ { i } = W ^ { V } x _ { i }$$

How does Self-Attention work?

Self-Attention

How does Self-Attention work?

To capture these three different roles, transformers introduce weight matrices used to project each input vector 𝑥𝑖 into a representation of its role as a key (K) , query (Q) , or value (V) .

How does Self-Attention work?

Self-Attention

How does Self-Attention work?

Self-Attention is carried out by the interaction of the queries, keys, and values matrices . Those are produced by multiplying the layer's inputs with the projection matrices

How does Self-Attention work?

Self-Attention

Scoring the relevance of context tokens is accomplished by multiplying the query associated with the current position with the keys matrix .

A typical approach is to divide the dot product by the square root of the dimensionality of the query and key vectors ( 𝑑𝑘 )

Scoring the relevance of context tokens is accomplished by multiplying the query associated with the current position with the keys matrix .

$$\boxed { \text {score} ( x _ { i } , x _ { j } ) \ = \ \frac { q _ { i } \cdot k _ { j } } { \sqrt { d _ { k } } } }$$

Scoring the relevance of context

concepts.title: Attention Scoring
concepts.summary: The relevance between context tokens is scored by multiplying the query of the current position with the keys matrix, followed by scaling.

concepts.title: Self-Attention Computation Formula
concepts.summary: The core computation for self-attention involves scaling the dot product of the Query (Q) and Key (K) matrices, applying softmax, and then multiplying by the Value (V) matrix.

grounding.text:
How does Self-Attention work?

$$y _ { i } \, = \, \sum _ { j \leq i } \alpha _ { i j } v _ { j }$$

How does Self-Attention work?

Self-Attention

Parallel Processing

Self-Attention

Parallel Processing

Since each output, 𝒚𝒊 , is computed independently this entire process can be parallelized by taking advantage of matrix multiplication by packing the input embeddings of the 𝑁 tokens of the input sequence into a single matrix:

Parallel Processing

$$X \, \in \, \mathbb { R } ^ { N \, \times \, d }$$

Parallel Processing

That is, each row of 𝑿 is the embedding of one token of the input.

Parallel Processing

Self-Attention

Parallel Processing

We then multiply 𝑋 by the key , query , and value matrices (all of dimensionality 𝑑 × 𝑑 ) to produce the following matrices containing all the key, query, and value vectors:

Parallel Processing

Self-Attention

Parallel Processing

We can compute all the requisite query-key comparisons simultaneously by multiplying 𝑸 and 𝑲 𝑻 in a single matrix multiplication

Parallel Processing

Self-Attention

Parallel Processing

We can scale these scores, take the softmax , and then multiply the result by 𝑉 .

Parallel Processing

Self-attention can be reduced for an entire sequence of N tokens to the following computation:

Parallel Processing

$$\text {SelfAttention} ( Q , K , V ) \, = \, \text {softmax} \left ( \frac { Q K ^ { \top } } { \sqrt { d _ { k } } } \right ) V$$

Parallel Processing

concepts.title: Positional Embeddings
concepts.summary: Since transformers inherently lack positional awareness, positional embeddings are added to the input embeddings to give the model information about the relative or absolute position of tokens.

concepts.title: Layer Normalization
concepts.summary: Layer normalization is a statistical technique applied to a single hidden layer, normalizing summed vectors using learnable gain ($\gamma$) and offset ($ eta$) parameters.

grounding.text:
Deeper into the Encoder

Residual connections pass information from a lower layer to a higher layer without going through the intermediate layer.

Deeper into the Encoder

This allows information from the activation going forward and the gradient going backwards to skip a layer.

Deeper into the Encoder

concepts.title: Token Generation Process
concepts.summary: LLMs generate text token by token, where each newly generated token is appended to the prompt and fed back into the model for the next forward pass.

grounding.text:
How Large Language Models Generate T ext

Transformer LLMs generate one token at a time , not the entire text at once.

How Large Language Models Generate T ext

An output token is appended to the prompt , then this new text is presented to the model again for another forward pass to generate the next token.

How Large Language Models Generate T ext

How do we turn the decoder's outputs into a word?

That's the job of the final Linear layer which is followed by a Softmax Layer .

How do we turn the decoder's outputs into a word?

The Linear layer is a simple neural network that projects the vector produced by the stack of decoders, into a much, much larger vector called a logits vector .

How Large Language Models Generate T ext

The Architecture of the Decoder Let's assume that our model knows 10,000 unique English words (our model's 'output vocabulary') that it's learned from its training dataset.

How Large Language Models Generate T ext

This would make the logits vector 10,000 cells wide -each cell corresponding to the score of a unique word. That is how we interpret the output of the model followed by the Linear layer.

The origin of Transformers

Transformer Architecture

The origin of Transformers

The standard architecture for building modern large language models is the Transformer .

The origin of Transformers

Like LSTMs, Transformers can capture long-distance relationships between words in a sequence. However, unlike LSTMs, Transformers do not rely on recurrent connections that process tokens step by step .

The origin of Transformers

Instead, Transformers use self-attention , a mechanism that allows each token to directly attend to all other tokens in the sequence. This makes it possible to compute token representations in parallel , improving training efficiency and making much better use of GPUs .

The Architecture behind Transformers

Transformer Architecture

The Architecture behind Transformers

Transformers are made of two main types of blocks : encoders and decoders.

The Architecture behind Transformers

The Encoder reads the input sequence and transforms each token into a contextual representation .

concepts.title: LLM Text Generation Procedure
concepts.summary: LLMs generate text iteratively by sampling a word from a softmax distribution based on the preceding text, starting with a beginning-of-sentence marker.

grounding.text:
How Large Language Models Generate T ext

The previous process is repeated until a special symbol (ex: </s>, [SEP], etc.) is reached indicating the transformer decoder has completed its output.

How Large Language Models Generate T ext

The Architecture of the Decoder

How Large Language Models Generate T ext

In summary, the procedure for generation from a Transformer Decoder is:

How Large Language Models Generate T ext

1. Sample a word in the output from the softmax distribution that results from using the beginning of sentence marker, <s>, as the first input.
2. Use the word embedding for that first word as the input to the network at the next time step and then sample the next word in the same fashion.
3. Continue generating until the end of sentence marker, </s>, is sampled or a fixed length limit is reached.

How Large Language Models Generate T ext

concepts.title: Local Generative AI Techniques
concepts.summary: Quantization reduces model size and memory usage by decreasing the bits used to represent parameters, enabling LLMs to run on consumer devices. GGUF is a format for compressed, quantized LLMs.

grounding.text:
How Large Language Models Generate T ext

The previous process is repeated until a special symbol (ex: </s>, [SEP], etc.) is reached indicating the transformer decoder has completed its output.

How Large Language Models Generate T ext

The Architecture of the Decoder

How Large Language Models Generate T ext

In summary, the procedure for generation from a Transformer Decoder is:

How Large Language Models Generate T ext

1. Sample a word in the output from the softmax distribution that results from using the beginning of sentence marker, <s>, as the first input.
2. Use the word embedding for that first word as the input to the network at the next time step and then sample the next word in the same fashion.
3. Continue generating until the end of sentence marker, </s>, is sampled or a fixed length limit is reached.

concepts.title: Temperature Parameter
concepts.summary: Temperature controls the likelihood of less probable tokens being generated in an LLM output. High temperatures encourage creativity, while low temperatures favor more predictable outputs.

grounding.text:

Should be high for brainstorming sessions or creative writing, while low for email generation and translation, for ex.

concepts.title: Top_p Sampling
concepts.summary: Top_p sampling selects tokens from the smallest set whose cumulative probability exceeds a given threshold p. Lowering p results in less creative output, while increasing it allows selection from more tokens.

concepts.title: LLM Limitations
concepts.summary: LLMs are trained on fixed datasets and cannot inherently know new, private, or organization-specific information after training, and they can sometimes generate incorrect answers (hallucinate).

grounding.text:
How can LLMs answer questions about your data?

LLMs are trained on massive amounts of data, but they cannot know new, private, or organizationspecific information after training . Sometimes, they may also 'hallucinate2 or generate incorrect answers when the required information is missing

Retrieval Augmented Generation

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an AI framework that grounds Large Language Models (LLMs) with specific, authoritative data outside their original training data.

Retrieval Augmented Generation

A basic RAG pipeline is made up of a search step followed by a grounded generation step where the LLM is prompted with the question and the information retrieved from the search step

concepts.title: Agentic AI
concepts.summary: Agentic AI uses LLMs as a cognitive core to understand problems, break them down, and autonomously use external tools to complete tasks.

grounding.text:
How can LLMs perform actions autonomously

On its own, a standalone LLM is primarily performing next-token prediction .

How can LLMs perform actions autonomously

Hence, LLMs can struggle with some tasks, including basic arithmetic , such as multiplication and division.

How can LLMs perform actions autonomously

Agentic AI

How can LLMs perform actions autonomously

LLM agents are advanced AI systems that use Large Language Models (LLMs) as their "brain" to understand problems, break them down into steps, and autonomously use tools (like APIs, databases, or code execution) to complete tasks.

How can LLMs perform actions autonomously

In Agentic AI , The LLM functions as the cognitive core , augmented by three primary components:

How can LLMs perform actions autonomously

- Memory : Divided into short-term (working memory managing recent interaction context) and long-term memory (external, persistent knowledge accessed via RAG).
- Tools : External interfaces (e.g., APIs) enabling real-time interaction with dynamic environments.
- Planning/Reasoning : The core generative capability of the LLM, which forms plans and generates responses.

How can LLMs perform actions autonomously

Agentic AI

How can LLMs perform actions autonomously

concepts.title: Tool Calling in Agentic AI
concepts.summary: Tools are external interfaces that allow LLMs to interact with dynamic environments, enabling the LLM to execute functions and use the output to formulate a response.

grounding.text:
How can LLMs perform actions autonomously

On its own, a standalone LLM is primarily performing next-token prediction .

How can LLMs perform actions autonomously

Hence, LLMs can struggle with some tasks, including basic arithmetic , such as multiplication and division.

How can LLMs perform actions autonomously

Agentic AI

How can LLMs perform actions autonomously

LLM agents are advanced AI systems that use Large Language Models (LLMs) as their "brain" to understand problems, break them down into steps, and autonomously use tools (like APIs, databases, or code execution) to complete tasks.

How can LLMs perform actions autonomously

In Agentic AI , The LLM functions as the cognitive core , augmented by three primary components:

How can LLMs perform actions autonomously

- Memory : Divided into short-term (working memory managing recent interaction context) and long-term memory (external, persistent knowledge accessed via RAG).
- Tools : External interfaces (e.g., APIs) enabling real-time interaction with dynamic environments.
- Planning/Reasoning : The core generative capability of the LLM, which forms plans and generates responses.

concepts.title: MindEval Process
concepts.summary: MindEval involves generating interactions with state-of-the-art LLMs, obtaining evaluations from psychologists and automatic judges, and comparing correlations between these judgments.

grounding.text:
An example of the effect of the prompt

GPT-5  Chat  without  formatting  instructions :  'If  I'm  being  practical about  it,  the  first  thing  I  should  probably  do  is  stop  talking  for  a  few seconds once I feel that heat coming up. Doesn't mean backing down, but giving myself that pause so I'm not cutting people off mid-sentence. I've noticed when I jump in too fast, it turns into a bigger clash than it needs to be. If I can let them finish-even if I already know where I stand-then I at least give them the space to feel heard before I drop my side of it.'

concepts.title: Future Improvements for MindEval
concepts.summary: Future work on MindEval focuses on improving patient simulation, moving scoring from a paradigm to rubrics, and mitigating biases in automatic judges.

grounding.text:
Remaining limitations

Much room for improvement in patient realism . Patients are too agreeable. Hard to strike balance between personality and style

Remaining limitations

No high-risk interactions . Therapy often implies dealing with high-risk patients. MindEval could be adapted for this, though

Remaining limitations

Absence of longitudinal dynamics . Many effects of therapy are not measured in a single interaction, but over weeks, months, years

Check out the paper and our repo!

Paper

Check out the paper and our repo!

Repo

Beyond MindEval

Patients are still 'too easy' : we are working actively on improving patient simulation through better models, better context engineering, and real data

Beyond MindEval

Beyond MindEval

MindEval scores are hard to interpret : we are moving from a scoring paradigm in the direction of rubrics (think of a pilot's checklist)

concepts.title: Market Sentiment
concepts.summary: Market sentiment describes investor attitudes, which can be bullish (expecting continued gains) or bearish (worrying that good times are ending).

grounding.text:
Text Mining

Spring Semester 2025/2026

Text Mining

Project Assignment Handout {version 2.0 - 'corrected date in Extra Information' section}

Text Mining

This handout details the rules for the mandatory practical project for Text Mining, to be developed and completed during the academic calendar of the Text Mining class.

1. Project Summary

'Over time, major indexes go up and down based on internal and external factors. Performance like that excites investors, but typically in opposite ways. Constant gains lead some investors to expect  more  of  the  same.  Others  worry  the  good  times  are  surely  about  to  end.  The  former sentiment is sometimes called 'bullish,' while the latter is referred to as 'bearish.' 1

1. Project Summary

The goal of this project is to develop an NLP model capable of predicting Market sentiment based on  tweets.  In  summary,  with  the  NLP  techniques  you  have  learned  during  class,  you  must implement a classification model that receives tweets as inputs and is able to predict, for each tweet, if it describes a Bearish (0), Bullish (1), or Neutral (2) attitude.

1. Project Summary

The project should be developed using python 3 and libraries such as NLTK , Scikit Learn , Hugging face and LangChain . Also, the project can be solved in various ways, which means there is no exact correct solution.

2. Group Rules

The project should be done in a group of between one (1) to four (4) students; we consider this the ideal size for group

This handout details the rules for the mandatory practical project for Text Mining, to be developed and completed during the academic calendar of the Text Mining class.

1. Project Summary

'Over time, major indexes go up and down based on internal and external factors. Performance like that excites investors, but typically in opposite ways. Constant gains lead some investors to expect  more  of  the  same.  Others  worry  the  good  times  are  surely  about  to  end.  The  former sentiment is sometimes called 'bullish,' while the latter is referred to as 'bearish.' 1

1. Project Summary

The goal of this project is to develop an NLP model capable of predicting Market sentiment based on  tweets.  In  summary,  with  the  NLP  techniques  you  have  learned  during  class,  you  must implement a classification model that receives tweets as inputs and is able to predict, for each tweet, if it describes a Bearish (0), Bullish (1), or Neutral (2) attitude.

1. Project Summary

The project should be developed using python 3 and libraries such as NLTK , Scikit Learn , Hugging face and LangChain . Also, the project can be solved in various ways, which means there is no exact correct solution.

2. Group Rules

The project should be done in a group of between one (1) to four (4) students; we consider this the ideal size for group

concepts.title: Data Exploration
concepts.summary: This step requires analyzing corpora to provide conclusions and visual information, such as bar charts or word clouds, that contextualize the data.

grounding.text:
4. Solution Requirements

1. Data Exploration 2.00 pts :  Here you should analyze the corpora and provide some conclusions and visual information (bar charts, word clouds, etc.) that contextualize the data.
2. Corpus split 0. 50 pts : You must apply some method to split your training corpus into train/validation sets to evaluate the performance of your model. You can also resort to K-Fold cross validation.
3. Data Preprocessing 3.00 pts : You must correctly implement at least four (4) of the data preprocessing techniques shown in class (stop words, regular expressions, lemmatization, stemming, etc.).
4. Feature Engineering 5.50 pts : You must correctly implement and experiment at least one variation of each  of  the  following  feature  engineering  techniques  seen  in  class:  BoW,  word2vec,  Transformer Encoder.
5. Classification Models 4.50 pts : You must correctly implement and test at least two variations of each the  following  classification  methods  seen  in  class,  including  Traditional  ML  (KNN,  MLP,  Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, etc.) and Transformer Encoders.
6. Evaluation and Analysis 1.50 pts : You must correctly evaluate and compare your models resorting, at least, to Recall, Precision, Accuracy and F1-Score, and explain what the evaluation means in the context of the problem.

concepts.title: Report Submission Guidelines
concepts.summary: The PDF report must not exceed 15 pages, and any extra work must be explicitly defined in the report to be considered for evaluation.

grounding.text:
Extra Information:

- The PDF report should have a maximum of 15 pages describing the previous points. Exceeding this number will incur a 0.5-point penalty for each extra page.
- Any extra work developed must be clearly defined as such in the PDF report ,  or  else it  will  not  be considered for evaluation as extra work!
- All files should be saved in a folder named 'group_xx'. This folder (zip it if you need) must be submitted through Moodle's project submission section until 23h:59 of the 15 th of June .
- Failure to deliver on time will incur a 1.0-point penalty for each half-day late.
- Failure to comply with the delivery guide above will meet with up to 1.0-point penalty .
- To prevent plagiarism and misuse of Gen AI, students may be randomly chosen for an oral defense to assess their understanding.

Extra Challenge 1

We will compare your predictions with the actual Label from the test set ('test.csv').

Extra Challenge 1

The three (3) groups with the highest performance will receive points as follows:

Extra Challenge 1

- 1.00 points for the group with the best model
- 0.50 points for the group with the 2 nd best model
- 0.25 points for the group with the 3 rd best model

grounding.text:
# Example questions:

##### 1. In a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN), what is the primary role of the hidden state?
A. It serves as the network's memory by capturing information from previous time steps
B. It stores the final output of the entire sequence for downstream classification
C. It replaces the input at each time step with the predicted output from the previous step
D. It accumulates gradients during backpropagation without influencing forward computation
Answer: A

##### 2. Which of the following is true?
A. BERT uses LSTMs
B. BERT is an autoregressive model
C. BERT is an encoder-based Transformer
D. BERT is a decoder-based Transformer
Answer: C

##### 3. What is the main step when calculating the attention score in a seq2seq model?
A. Taking the dot product between the encoder and decoder hidden states
B. Averaging the encoder hidden states
C. Applying a softmax function to the decoder hidden states
D. Concatenating the encoder hidden states and passing them through a linear layer
Answer: A
grounding.citation: sample questions

concepts.title: NLP Foundations and Goals
concepts.summary: The course introduces NLP foundations, aiming for students to understand text preparation, feature extraction, and applying various models for text analysis.

grounding.text:
Text Mining

Course Presentation

Course Mission

Text Mining

Course Mission

This course introduces the foundations and applications of Natural Language Processing (NLP) methods . By the end of the course, students will:

Course Mission

- Understand how to prepare and represent text data through key preprocessing techniques (e.g., tokenization, stemming, lemmatization) and feature extraction methods like Bag of Words, TF-IDF, and word embeddings .
- Apply traditional and deep learning models to analyze and classify text , including logistic regression, LSTMs, Transformers, and Large Language Models (LLMs).
- Explore traditional Text Mining/NLP tasks, including sentiment analysis, document classification and topic modeling .
- Gain hands-on experience through lab sessions and a final project , using tools such as scikit-learn, Hugging Face Transformers, and LangChain to build practical NLP applications , including chatbots.

Teachers :

Bruno Jardim (bjardim@novaims.unl.pt)

Teachers :

Ricardo Dona (rdona@novaims.unl.pt)

Teachers :

Catarina Nascimento (cnascimento@novaims.unl.pt)

Office hours:

Please see with your teacher, to get his office hours

Faculty Staff

Text Mining

W1: Introduction to NLP

Introduction to text preprocessing and classification using Bag of Words models.

W2: Word Representations

Explore word vector representations (e.g., Word2Vec, GloVe) and apply them in classification.

W3: Sequential Models

Sequential models (RNNs, LSTMs) and applications
grounding.citation: Text Mining > Course Mission > Teachers : > Office hours: > Faculty Staff > W1: Introduction to NLP > W2: Word Representations > W3: Sequential Models > W4: Lar

concepts.title: NLP Tasks
concepts.summary: Students will explore common NLP tasks, including sentiment analysis, document classification, and topic modeling.

grounding.text:
Text Mining

Course Presentation

Course Mission

Text Mining

Course Mission

This course introduces the foundations and applications of Natural Language Processing (NLP) methods . By the end of the course, students will:

Course Mission

- Understand how to prepare and represent text data through key preprocessing techniques (e.g., tokenization, stemming, lemmatization) and feature extraction methods like Bag of Words, TF-IDF, and word embeddings .
- Apply traditional and deep learning models to analyze and classify text , including logistic regression, LSTMs, Transformers, and Large Language Models (LLMs).
- Explore traditional Text Mining/NLP tasks, including sentiment analysis, document classification and topic modeling .
- Gain hands-on experience through lab sessions and a final project , using tools such as scikit-learn, Hugging Face Transformers, and LangChain to build practical NLP applications , including chatbots.
